title: Matelea rupestris
intro: Matelea rupestris, also known as the "climbing milk vine," is a perennial plant species in the Apocynaceae family, characterized by its woody vines and clusters of small, white, funnel-shaped flowers. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, particularly its potential to reduce inflammation and alleviate anxiety, thanks to its adaptogenic and antioxidant effects. Matelea rupestris contains a range of bioactive constituents, including alkaloids such as norbelladine and norbelladine glycosides, which are believed to contribute to its therapeutic properties. In herbal medicine, Matelea rupestris is often prepared as a tincture or infusion, and is used to promote relaxation, calm the nervous system, and support the body's natural response to stress.
taxonomy: Matelea rupestris is classified in the kingdom Plantae, specifically within the family Apocynaceae. This species falls under the genus Matelea. Matelea rupestris is native to the Americas and is often found growing in rocky or mountainous areas. It is a type of vine or climbing plant that has adapted to thrive in these environments. The exact classification of Matelea rupestris may vary depending on the source, but it is generally accepted to be within the Apocynaceae family.
